description:
You are the lead web-developer tasked with producing a quick traffic snapshot from yesterday’s access logs that live under
   /home/user/project/logs/ .
 An automated monitor will later ingest your report files, so their **format and exact location must be respected to the
 character**.

 OBJECTIVE
 ─────────
 1. Read every line of every *.log* file currently found inside /home/user/project/logs/ .
 2. Extract the IPv4 address that appears at the very beginning of each line (everything up to, but **not including**, the
    first space).
 3. Count how many times every distinct IPv4 address occurs across the whole, combined data set.
 4. Produce two report files inside /home/user/project/reports/ (create the directory if it does not yet exist).

 REQUIRED OUTPUT FILES
 ─────────────────────
 A) /home/user/project/reports/ip_frequency.tsv  
    • plain text, UTF-8  
    • the **first line must be the fixed header**  
          hits<TAB>ip_address  
      (where <TAB> is the literal tab character 0x09).  
    • Every subsequent line represents one unique IPv4 address.  
      Format of each data line:  
          <hit_count><TAB><IPv4>  
    • Sort order:
        1. Descending (numeric) by <hit_count> (highest number first).  
        2. When two addresses have the same hit count, sort those ties in **ascending
           lexical order of the IPv4 string** (e.g. 10.0.0.3 comes before 172.16.0.1).  
    • Do NOT include any blank lines at the end of the file.

 B) /home/user/project/reports/top_5_ips.txt  
    • plain text, UTF-8  
    • Each line contains **only** an IPv4 address (no counts, no extra whitespace).  
    • The addresses must appear in the very same order as the first five data
      lines of ip_frequency.tsv.  
    • If fewer than five distinct IPs exist, list all of them and stop.  
    • No header, no trailing blank line.

 HINTS FOR YOUR SHELL SESSION (not mandatory rules, merely background):
 • Typical pipelines for this task revolve around “cat”, “grep -oE”, “sort -t”, “uniq -c”, “sort -k” etc.  
 • GNU sort/uniq support “--parallel” and “&” backgrounding if you want to speed things up, but only the final file
   contents will be tested, not the specific commands you ran.

 VERIFICATION CONDITIONS
 ───────────────────────
 Automated tests will confirm:

 1. The directory /home/user/project/reports/ exists.
 2. The file ip_frequency.tsv exists, is non-empty, and its first line is **exactly**  
        hits<TAB>ip_address  
    (case, spelling, spacing, and trailing newline all matter).
 3. The data lines of ip_frequency.tsv are correctly tab-separated, correctly sorted, and contain the right counts.
 4. The file top_5_ips.txt exists, is non-empty, contains no more than five lines,
    each line is a valid IPv4 address with no surrounding whitespace, and the ordering mirrors the first up-to-five data
    lines of ip_frequency.tsv.

 You do **not** need root privileges.  Do everything as the normal user.  When you are done,
 both report files must be present at the exact paths given above and pass the formatting rules verbatim.
truth:
Initial file-system state before the agent starts:

 1. Directory /home/user/project/logs/ already exists and is readable.
 2. It contains exactly two files:

    a) /home/user/project/logs/access1.log  
       10 lines:  
       192.168.0.1 - - [10/Jun/2023:10:15:32 +0000] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 1024  
       192.168.0.2 - - [10/Jun/2023:10:15:35 +0000] "POST /login HTTP/1.1" 302 512  
       192.168.0.1 - - [10/Jun/2023:10:15:38 +0000] "GET /style.css HTTP/1.1" 200 2048  
       10.0.0.5     - - [10/Jun/2023:10:15:40 +0000] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 1024  
       192.168.0.1 - - [10/Jun/2023:10:15:42 +0000] "GET /script.js HTTP/1.1" 200 4096  
       172.16.0.3 - - [10/Jun/2023:10:15:45 +0000] "GET /about HTTP/1.1" 200 512  
       192.168.0.2 - - [10/Jun/2023:10:15:48 +0000] "GET /dashboard HTTP/1.1" 200 1024  
       192.168.0.2 - - [10/Jun/2023:10:15:50 +0000] "GET /api/data HTTP/1.1" 200 256  
       10.0.0.5     - - [10/Jun/2023:10:15:52 +0000] "GET /style.css HTTP/1.1" 200 2048  
       10.0.0.5     - - [10/Jun/2023:10:15:55 +0000] "GET /script.js HTTP/1.1" 200 4096  

    b) /home/user/project/logs/access2.log  
       10 lines:  
       172.16.0.3 - - [11/Jun/2023:11:10:12 +0000] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 1024  
       192.168.0.1 - - [11/Jun/2023:11:10:14 +0000] "GET /contact HTTP/1.1" 200 512  
       192.168.0.2 - - [11/Jun/2023:11:10:16 +0000] "GET /products HTTP/1.1" 200 2048  
       192.168.0.2 - - [11/Jun/2023:11:10:18 +0000] "GET /products HTTP/1.1" 200 2048  
       10.0.0.5     - - [11/Jun/2023:11:10:20 +0000] "GET /checkout HTTP/1.1" 200 1024  
       172.16.0.3 - - [11/Jun/2023:11:10:22 +0000] "GET /cart HTTP/1.1" 200 512  
       172.16.0.3 - - [11/Jun/2023:11:10:24 +0000] "GET /cart HTTP/1.1" 200 512  
       10.0.0.5     - - [11/Jun/2023:11:10:26 +0000] "GET /api/data HTTP/1.1" 200 256  
       10.0.0.5     - - [11/Jun/2023:11:10:28 +0000] "POST /api/checkout HTTP/1.1" 200 512  
       10.0.0.5     - - [11/Jun/2023:11:10:30 +0000] "GET /thankyou HTTP/1.1" 200 1024  

 3. The directory /home/user/project/reports/ does NOT yet exist.

 The correct final content that the tests will look for:

 • /home/user/project/reports/ip_frequency.tsv  
   hits<TAB>ip_address\n  
   7<TAB>10.0.0.5\n  
   5<TAB>192.168.0.2\n  
   4<TAB>172.16.0.3\n  
   4<TAB>192.168.0.1\n

   (where \n denotes a single LF line-ending; there is exactly **one newline** after the last data line)

 • /home/user/project/reports/top_5_ips.txt  
   10.0.0.5\n  
   192.168.0.2\n  
   172.16.0.3\n  
   192.168.0.1\n

   (again, one LF after the last line)

 The tests will ignore file-system time-stamps and the specific shell commands executed; only the existence, exact paths,
 and exact file contents shown above are enforced.
